channel.title: Yale Climate Connections
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Yale Climate Connections

item.title: Simple tweaks can make wastewater treatment less polluting
item.description:
Transcript:

Wastewater treatment plants remove pollutants from sewage before it returns to lakes, rivers, and oceans.

But that treatment process can produce some harmful pollution of its own.

Jose Porro is CEO of Cobalt Water Global, a company that helps water utilities reduce their nitrous oxide emissions. Nitrous oxide is a powerful global warming gas.

Porro: “Nitrous oxide is also an ozone-depleting gas.”

And it can be created as an unintended byproduct of wastewater treatment, during the removal of nitrogen.

Nitrogen is present in people’s urine and has to be removed because if it ends up in waterways, it can cause harmful algae growth and other problems.

But under certain conditions, the microbes that break down nitrogen can produce nitrous oxide.

Porro says the problem can often be fixed through simple tweaks – for example, aerating the water less at certain times so less oxygen gets in.

So his company uses machine learning to help utilities monitor and fine-tune their processes.

Porro: “We’ve been able to achieve up to 80 to 90% reductions in the N2O emissions from making simple process adjustments.”

So water treatment plants can clean the water – without adding as much pollution to the atmosphere.

Reporting credit: Sarah Kennedy / ChavoBart Digital Media
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Tropical Storm Bertha makes its first landfall in southeastern Louisiana
item.description:
Update (Wednesday, July 22, 3:30 p.m. EDT):

Tropical Storm Bertha has officially made landfall near the Biloxi Wildlife Management Area in St. Bernard Parish, Louisiana, around 2 p.m. CDT, as announced in a special statement put out by the NHC. Bertha is about 40 miles east of New Orleans and continues to move west-northwest at 7 mph (11 km/h). Gusty winds are expected to increase along the warned areas.

Original article, posted on Wednesday, July 22:

Tropical Storm Bertha is approaching the southeastern coast of Louisiana as it moves slowly west-northwest. The 2 p.m. EDT Wednesday, July 22, advisory indicated that Bertha holds maximum sustained winds of 45 mph (75 km/h).

Tropical Storm Warnings are in effect for the metropolitan New Orleans area and the Lake Pontchartrain region. You can expect tropical storm conditions, meaning winds that could exceed 39 mph, to remain in place at least through the overnight hours Wednesday into Thursday. It is likely that the Tropical Storm Watches that are in effect across the central and western coast of Louisiana will be upgraded to Tropical Storm Warnings during the next advisories.

Keep in mind that Bertha, although it’s expected to increase its forward speed a little, will continue to move slower than average, at under 10 mph, through the next two days. This will be an all-week event as the system continues to brush the Louisiana coast throughout Wednesday and into early Thursday.

Hurricane Hunters indicated that Bertha’s center of circulation could be weakening, and it is possible that a new center of circulation could form closer to the cluster of thunderstorms that has been shifted mainly over water. So as Bertha continues to move slowly west and its center emerges over water, it will likely remain a tropical storm and not completely dissipate. However, it will be battered by wind shear, which means that Bertha is not likely to intensify before arriving in Texas near Galveston, likely on Thursday afternoon.

Once Bertha makes landfall, it will be the second storm this season to form and to make landfall in the Gulf Coast. Arthur made landfall on June 17 in southern Louisiana, bringing torrential rains that may have set a new all-time 24-hour precipitation record for the state: 29.06 inches (738 millimeters) near Cottonport, Louisiana. According to Gallagher Re, Arthur is responsible for at least $1 billion in damage and four deaths.

An unusual storm

Bertha has demonstrated that it’s not a typical tropical storm. With a strong high-pressure system to the north and very strong wind shear coming in from the north-northeast, much of the convection, which runs in the troposphere between 6,500 and 16,000 feet (2,000 and 5,000 meters) high, has been shifted over the open waters of the Gulf. Yes, there have been some rain showers and thunderstorms pushing across Florida and the Southeast, but these have been intermittent.

The biggest issue with Bertha has been the gusty winds that have brought very rough surf conditions and storm surge along the immediate coast. Panama City Beach recorded a sustained wind of 42 mph on Tuesday, and gusts above 50 mph were recorded along the Panhandle Coast. Storm surge will likely produce flooding along the immediate Gulf Coast east of the storm center; up to three feet of storm surge is possible, especially when the winds and location coincide with high tide.

The latest satellite image shows some breaks in the clouds across the Florida Panhandle, with the deepest convection moving slightly ahead of the system but still to the southwest of the center and over the Gulf. The winds are coming in directly from the south across the western portion of Florida through the Mississippi and Alabama border as the system’s center crosses southeastern Louisiana.

Central Texas Coast will likely be most impacted by Bertha’s rain

The heaviest cluster of thunderstorms associated with Bertha, which has been shifted well south of the center of the storm, will likely impact the central coast of Texas, extending from areas just south of Galveston County through the Corpus Christi area on Thursday afternoon and evening. Expect rainfall along the immediate central coast of Texas to range between two and four inches, while the greater Houston area will likely receive less than an inch of rain.

As far as the winds, they will remain around tropical storm strength across the central and western coast of Louisiana through Thursday afternoon. As the system moves closer to southeast Texas, the winds will start to diminish across Louisiana and likely pick up across the Galveston Bay area later Thursday afternoon as the system’s center approaches. The system will quickly dissipate as it moves over the Lone Star State.

It is quite rare for the first two named storms of the season to make a U.S. landfall. The last time this occurred was also during a strong El Niño year, 2015:

· Tropical Storm Ana (May 10): Made landfall near Myrtle Beach, South Carolina, becoming the earliest U.S. tropical cyclone landfall on record at the time.

· Tropical Storm Bill (June 16): Made landfall along the Texas coast near Port O’Connor.

Over the past 30 years, the U.S. has averaged just over three landfalls by named storms per year, with two of those landfalls being from hurricanes.

Has Bertha brought dangerous heat to the South?

Over 55 million people are under some sort of heat advisory across the U.S., but the highest concentration is over the South Central and Deep South. Bertha has ignited some interesting contrasts in warnings along the Gulf Coast on Wednesday. As our colleague Michael Lowry highlighted on his Substack Wednesday morning, it is very unusual to see heat advisories and extreme heat warnings in effect over the same areas that are being impacted by a tropical storm and are also under tropical storm warnings. This clearly shows how different Bertha has been from a typical tropical storm.

We have seen this mixture of warnings because strong wind shear has kept the deepest convection well away from the center and to the south. In most other situations, a tropical storm would bring a broader, deeper layer of clouds, which would keep temperatures below the threshold needed to trigger heat alerts. But with Bertha, the strong wind shear shifting that thick cloud layer away from land has allowed temperatures to soar. Heat index values could rise as high as 115°F through 7 p.m. CDT Wednesday across many areas of the Deep South. The heat alerts extend widely across the Deep South and westward over more than half of eastern Texas, reaching as far north as southern Oklahoma. A frontal boundary extends from the Appalachians through Arkansas and into southern Oklahoma, keeping all of the heat bottled up from Mississippi westward through much of Texas.

Read: The emerging danger of post-hurricane heat waves

Is there anything else brewing in the Atlantic basin?

Aside from Tropical Storm Bertha, the National Hurricane Center does not have anything else highlighted across the Atlantic basin for the next seven days.

The latest satellite imagery shows two tropical waves emerging from Africa, one ahead of the Cabo Verde Islands and another deeper cluster of thunderstorms just southeast of the Cabo Verde Islands. However, these systems are not likely to develop within the next seven days. The rest of the central and western Atlantic continues to be dominated by Saharan dust, which will bring hazy skies across Puerto Rico, the Dominican Republic, Haiti, and eastern Cuba over the weekend.

Eastern Pacific stays active, but no immediate threats to land

Of the two named storms in the Atlantic plus six in the Eastern Pacific this season, just one has made it to hurricane strength thus far: Hurricane Fausto, which is currently churning through the remote Northeast Pacific between Mexico and Hawaii after reaching Category 1 strength on Monday evening, July 20. That’s a full three weeks later than the average arrival date of the first Eastern Pacific hurricane, even though the region is running several weeks ahead of schedule on named storms.

As of 11 a.m. EDT Wednesday, Fausto was centered about 850 miles west-southwest of Cabo San Lucas, Mexico, moving west-northwest at 7 mph (11 km/h) with top sustained winds of 80 mph (130 km/h). Fausto’s pulsing central core of showers and thunderstorms (convection) was accompanied by a broader shield of convection extending well to the east. Wind shear is moderate (10 knots), and sea surface temperatures along Fausto’s path are just 1 degree Celsius above the typical hurricane-sustaining threshold of 26°C (79°F),  so Fausto appears likely to maintain its strength over several days and could intensify slightly at times. However, NHC was not predicting Fausto to reach Category 2 before it encounters gradually cooler waters and a steadily drier atmosphere. The latter will bring midlevel relative humidity down from about 75% on Wednesday to around 50% by the coming weekend.

The model consensus is for Fausto to pass just north of the Hawaiian Islands early next week as a weakening tropical storm, though some models – including some members of the Google DeepMind ensemble, the top performer on Atlantic tropical cyclone tracks in 2025 – suggest Fausto or its remnants could pass directly over the islands.

Jeff Masters and Bob Henson contributed to this post.

MORE LIKE THIS

Summer heat could be hurting your heart without you knowing it

Tropical Storm Bertha is born, still crawling in the Gulf, with strong wind shear impacting it

You can save lives this hurricane season
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Apoya nuestra cobertura bilingüe de huracanes.
item.description:
Me hice meteoróloga porque sé que, cuando un huracán amenaza, las personas en peligro necesitan información confiable, veraz, práctica y de inmediato. Me uní al equipo de Yale Climate Connections para ayudar a poner esa información que puede salvar vidas al alcance de los lectores hispanohablantes en los EE. UU., porque con demasiada frecuencia no pueden contar con las noticias locales para recibir actualizaciones sobre tormentas en su idioma preferido.

De hecho, más del 70 % de nuestros lectores hispanohablantes afirma que nuestra cobertura les ayuda a mantenerse seguros durante el clima extremo.

Por eso les pedimos a lectores como tú que nos ayuden a mantener y hacer crecer nuestra cobertura del clima extremo. ¿Te convertirás en donante hoy?

Dona ahora

Como beneficio adicional, cada dólar que dones durante este período será igualado, hasta $10,000, gracias al generoso apoyo de un lector anónimo de Yale Climate Connections.

Para agradecerte tu apoyo, cuando te conviertas en donante mensual, recibirás una invitación para participar en una conversación exclusiva —solo para donantes mensuales— por Zoom más adelante en la temporada, junto al meteorólogo Jeff Masters, la editora en jefe Sara Peach y yo.

Dona ahora

Cuando llegues a la página de donación, asegúrate de seleccionar la opción “recurrente” y recuerda que, si configuras tu donación como anónima, no tendremos tu información para invitarte a nuestra conversación por Zoom. El 1 de agosto es el último día para calificar.

Si prefieres donar por cheque, hazlo a nombre de “Yale University” y escribe “For Yale Climate Connections” en el cheque. Envíalo por correo a: Yale Climate Connections, Yale University, Attn: Development Office, 205 Prospect Street, New Haven, CT 06511

Yale University es una organización sin fines de lucro 501(c)3. Su número de identificación fiscal federal es 06-0646973.

Gracias por tu apoyo mientras nos preparamos para la temporada alta de huracanes,

Irene Sans

Meteoróloga y escritora
Yale Climate Connections
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: The coffee shop and barber shop that stay open when the power goes out
item.description:
Transcript:

On the west side of Salt Lake City, customers stop in at Culture Coffee for caffeine and conversation.

Stone: “We have all our clients that come in and get coffee and relax and hang out. And it’s really just a safe space for all different walks of life.”

That’s Eric Stone. He and Isaac Atencio co-own the cafe and adjoining barber shop, Salt Lake Barber Company. With their businesses, they work to build and support community.

And during a neighborhood power outage, they can continue doing so thanks to rooftop solar panels and batteries that keep the electricity running.

Atencio: “We could be handing ice out the doors, or we could have air conditioning running if we needed. We could also have a place for people to charge their phones.”

The businesses worked with the nonprofit Utah Clean Energy on the solar and battery project, which was funded through grant money and tax credits.

And Atencio says generating their own clean energy is also saving them a lot of money on their utility bills.

Atencio: “Ultimately it has been a real great boon to us from both a bottom-line perspective, but also another way where we get to live our mission and show that we really believe in sustainability and also, you know, be a hub of resiliency in the event of sustained power outages for the neighborhood.”

Reporting credit: Sarah Kennedy / ChavoBart Digital Media
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Tormenta Tropical Bertha avanza lentamente por el Golfo, fuerte cizalladura del viento está afectando su desarrollo
item.description:
[Click here to read in English]

El Centro Nacional de Huracanes oficialmente nombró la depresión tropical dos como Tormenta Tropical Bertha en el boletín de las 8:00 p. m. EDT el lunes 20 de junio.

Los aviones cazahuracanes de la NOAA investigaron el sistema durante toda la tarde y confirmaron que la depresión se había fortalecido hasta convertirse en una tormenta tropical. Ya para el martes en la tarde, Bertha alcanza vientos máximos sostenidos de 60 mph (96 km/h) mientras continúa desplazándose lentamente hacia el oeste-noroeste a aproximadamente 5 mph (8 km/h). Se espera que Bertha siga como tormenta tropical, ya que hay viento cortante en altura que limita su fortalecimiento. Los cazahuracanes observaron que el centro del sistema había mostrado un movimiento errático durante gran parte del día debido a su ubicación entre dos altas presiones de nivel medio de la atmósfera. Se realizarán nuevos vuelos de reconocimiento durante la noche, con tres misiones adicionales programadas entre el martes y el miércoles.

Debido a este comportamiento errático, aún podrían producirse pequeños cambios en la trayectoria pronosticada. Además, una fuerte cizalladura del viento continúa afectando a Bertha y seguirá limitando su fortalecimiento, manteniendo por ahora las lluvias más intensas al sur del centro del sistema. Continuaremos vigilando de cerca su evolución.

Bertha se formó apenas tres días después de la fecha promedio de la segunda tormenta con nombre de la temporada atlántica (17 de julio), tomando como referencia el período 1991-2020. En contraste, el Pacífico Oriental produjo su sexta tormenta de 2026, Fausto, el domingo 19 de julio, más de dos semanas antes de lo previsto.

Recuerde que el sistema se desplazará muy lentamente. Como resultado, los vientos de tormenta tropical aumentarán gradualmente a lo largo del martes en las costas de Mississippi y Alabama. Para la noche del martes, las ráfagas se intensificarán a medida que el sistema se aproxime al sureste de Louisiana, especialmente en la parroquia de Plaquemines.

Patrón atmosférico mantiene a Bertha avanzando lentamente

El sistema de alta presión ubicado sobre el centro de Estados Unidos continuará guiando a Bertha principalmente hacia el oeste durante esta semana. Otra zona de alta presión sobre el Atlántico occidental impide que el sistema sea arrastrado hacia el norte por la vaguada que se extiende por el este de Estados Unidos. Al quedar atrapada entre ambas áreas de alta presión, la depresión tropical presenta una muy débil corriente de dirección.

Durante el fin de semana, el sistema permaneció prácticamente estacionario y, hasta la mañana del lunes, avanzaba hacia el noroeste a apenas 3 mph (5 km/h) y, aunque se ha mantenido lento, el sistema debería acelerar y realizar un giro más marcado hacia el oeste. El cono de incertidumbre del Centro Nacional de Huracanes, emitido el martes por la tarde, indica que el centro de Bertha seguirá abrazando la zona costera central del Golfo de México, específicamente el estado de Louisiana, y seguirá moviéndose muy lentamente, a menos de 8 mph. Las aguas cerca de la costa de Louisiana oscilan entre 85 y 87 °F (29 a 30 °C) y, aunque esto podría servir de combustible para que una tormenta se fortalezca, la cizalladura del viento será tan fuerte que no permitirá que el sistema se alimente de estas aguas cálidas.

Hay que tener presente que el sistema probablemente ampliará su campo de viento ligeramente a medida que avanza lentamente. Las tormentas que enfrentan la entrada de aire seco suelen compensarla aumentando su tamaño. Además, el sistema continuará ganando latitud durante los próximos días, por lo que los vientos persistentes del mar hacia tierra seguirán generando fuerte oleaje y condiciones marítimas peligrosas a lo largo de la costa oeste de Florida hasta mediados de la semana.

Impactos a la costa, mayormente

El movimiento hacia el oeste-noroeste también mantendrá vientos persistentes desde el Panhandle de Florida hasta la costa central del Golfo durante buena parte de la semana.

Como consecuencia, la marejada ciclónica, combinada con varios ciclos de marea alta, podría provocar inundaciones costeras al empujar el agua tierra adentro. Desde el Panhandle de Florida hasta la costa central del Golfo, incluyendo Mobile Bay, podrían registrarse niveles de marejada de hasta 3 pies (0,9 metros); en algunas zonas, hasta 4 pies.

El lento desplazamiento del sistema también favorecerá algunos períodos de lluvias intensas en los estados de la costa del Golfo. En algunas zonas aisladas, los acumulados podrían superar las 3 pulgadas (75 mm) a lo largo de la semana. La mayor parte de los chubascos más intensos, que producen acumulados más altos, se mantendrá sobre el mar, ya que los vientos fuertes del noreste empujan las tormentas, alejándolas de tierra.

Las lluvias que necesitábamos se esfuman

Esperábamos que, cuando la cizalladura del viento no se veía tan fuerte, hubiese más lluvias sobre algunas secciones del sur. Zonas como Louisiana y Alabama todavía están con un déficit de lluvias, y parece que esta tormenta no aliviará esto. Mucho menos en la Florida, donde se necesita mucha más lluvia para aliviar el gran déficit existente.

Según el más reciente mapa de Terminación y Alivio de la Sequía de la NOAA, el sur de Louisiana necesita entre 7 y 11 pulgadas (180 a 280 mm) de lluvia en un solo mes para salir de la sequía prolongada, mientras que el Panhandle de Florida requiere alrededor de 14 pulgadas (355 mm). La región que más necesita lluvia es la península de Florida, donde serían necesarias entre 22 y 25 pulgadas (560 a 635 mm) en un mes para eliminar la sequía de largo plazo.

¿Es inusual que una tormenta se desplace hacia el oeste a través del Golfo de México?

Siempre que el patrón atmosférico lo favorezca, los ciclones tropicales pueden desplazarse en direcciones que, a primera vista, parecen poco habituales.

Para que un sistema avance hacia el oeste —en lugar de seguir la trayectoria más común hacia el noroeste o recurvar hacia el noreste—, normalmente debe existir una fuerte dorsal o alta presión subtropical ubicada al norte de la tormenta. Esta dorsal impide que el ciclón realice su giro natural hacia el norte y, al mismo tiempo, las débiles corrientes de dirección obligan al sistema a desplazarse aún más hacia el oeste.

Bertha pasará a formar parte de una lista relativamente corta de ciclones que se han formado en el este del Golfo de México y luego han seguido una trayectoria hacia el oeste.

Uno de los ejemplos más recientes fue la Tormenta Tropical Fernand en 2019. Aunque no se formó en el este del Golfo, siguió un recorrido similar hacia el oeste antes de tocar tierra en el noreste de México el 4 de septiembre de 2019.

Un caso aún más parecido fue el Huracán Erika en 2003, que cruzó gran parte del Golfo de México de este a oeste. Erika se formó el 14 de agosto a partir de los remanentes de un frente estacionario, se fortaleció hasta convertirse en huracán el 15 de agosto y tocó tierra cerca de Tamaulipas, México, el 16 de agosto como un huracán de Categoría 1, con vientos máximos sostenidos de 75 mph (120 km/h).

Jeff Masters y Bob Henson contribuyeron a este artículo.

Más artículos:

Apoya nuestra cobertura bilingüe de huracanes.

Tormenta Tropical Bertha avanza lentamente por el Golfo, fuerte cizalladura del viento está afectando su desarrollo

El Pacífico oriental es una fábrica de tormentas tropicales, y la cuenca del Atlántico muestra señales de vida.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Summer heat could be hurting your heart without you knowing it
item.description:
Sadashiv Yadav, 46, collapsed from a heart attack in 2024 after working long hours in western India’s sorghum and soybean fields, where heat rises in the summer to over 40°C (104°F). Yadav realized he was in trouble when he felt a sharp pain in his chest, and sweat poured down his body. 

“Even in peak summers, I hadn’t experienced that kind of sweating,” he recalled.

At the hospital, doctors found blockages in the arteries supplying blood to his heart and surmised that years of working in extreme heat likely strained his cardiovascular system. 

Many forms of heart disease are linked to chronic inflammation, and scientists are probing whether global warming has worsened this condition for people around the world, even among younger adults in northern Europe where extreme heat is much less common than in India.

Unlike acute inflammation, the immune system’s tool to fight infection or heal an injury, chronic inflammation is a prolonged immune response that can damage healthy tissues over time. Chronic inflammation has been linked to conditions ranging from arthritis to heart disease, diabetes, stroke, and other major illnesses.

“People often think heat is only dangerous when it causes heat stroke or during a heat wave, but that is probably too narrow,” said environmental epidemiologist Daniel Riggs, who is an assistant professor in the division of environmental medicine and the Christina Lee Brown Envirome Institute at the University of Louisville. 

Emerging evidence suggests that even routine exposure to elevated temperatures may alter biological markers linked to inflammation, immune responses, and the functioning of blood vessels.

Cardiovascular diseases are the world’s leading killer, claiming an estimated 19.8 million lives in 2022. Around 85% of these deaths resulted from heart attacks and strokes, with more than three-quarters occurring in low-and middle-income countries.

How heat fuels inflammation

Recent studies have revealed evidence of these subtle biological changes. In Louisville, Kentucky, researchers analyzed blood samples from 624 adults and linked hotter conditions, measured using a heat metric that accounted for temperature, humidity, and wind, with signs of increased inflammation and measurable changes in the immune system.

Hotter conditions were associated with nearly 10% higher levels of some immune cells involved in the body’s inflammatory response, while levels of TNF-alpha, a molecule that helps drive inflammation, were about 7% higher.

The study did not track disease outcomes, yet the researchers say the findings raise concerns that repeated exposure to high temperatures could alter immune responses and make people more vulnerable to infections. Over time, these changes may also increase the risk of cardiovascular disease.

Riggs, who co-authored the study, found that even short-term exposure to heat appeared to affect both blood vessels and the immune system. Changes associated with inflammation included the cells lining blood vessels becoming more reactive, while immune cells were more likely to attach to vessel walls. The study also suggested that extreme heat may influence how immune cells communicate and respond to stress.

In a large Chinese study that examined some 3,000 proteins in nearly 4,000 adults, exposure to temperatures around 29.5°C (85.1°F) was associated with increases in some proteins and decreases in others, affecting the levels of 770 proteins, many of which were involved in inflammation, immune responses, and blood clotting. Many of these proteins were also linked to blood pressure and future risk of heart disease.

Riggs explained that repeated exposure to high temperatures and temperature fluctuations may gradually affect the lining of blood vessels, disrupt normal immune function, and increase the tendency for blood clot formation. These are all biological pathways linked to cardiovascular disease. 

A climate change connection

Scientists are beginning to uncover biological clues hinting that cardiovascular problems could become more common in a warming world. In Germany, researchers analyzed blood samples from 359 women with an average age of 74. They examined temperature exposures over periods ranging from a few days to two weeks before the samples were collected. 

A 5°C rise in ambient temperature was associated with roughly 10% higher levels of inflammatory molecules involved in immune regulation and the body’s response to stress. Some of them have previously been linked to cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and other chronic conditions.

The risks may increase when heat interacts with other environmental stressors. 

“People experiencing co-exposures, especially air pollutants like PM2.5, may also be at higher risk because heat and pollution can act on overlapping inflammatory and vascular pathways,” Riggs said. PM2.5 refers to tiny air pollution particles that can be inhaled deep into the lungs.

Zhebin Yu, an environmental epidemiologist and assistant professor at Sweden’s Karolinska Institute, added that regulators should also take steps to reduce air pollution, which also worsens inflammation.

Wildfires, which are also growing much more common as the climate warms, may offer a glimpse of what happens when multiple environmental stressors converge. In Portugal, scientists reported higher levels of inflammation and signs of elevated cardiovascular risk among wildland firefighters after wildfire exposure. Although it could not disentangle the effects of heat from wildfire smoke, physical exertion, and other hazards associated with firefighting, it adds to evidence that climate-related exposures can activate inflammatory pathways.

Not just an older person’s problem

Although older adults are vulnerable to heat, emerging evidence suggests they are not the only ones affected. In Sweden, researchers followed 807 participants with a mean age of about 26 years and collected blood samples on three occasions between 2020 and 2022. 

The team examined 365 inflammation-related proteins and compared them with short-term outdoor temperature exposures estimated at each participant’s home address. Some 58 proteins, about 16% of those studied, varied with temperature exposure. Many of them were involved in immune responses and inflammation, and several were also linked to measures of lung function, blood pressure, and blood sugar control. Exposure to both unusually hot and cold temperatures was associated with signs of accelerated biological aging. 

“Many believe temperature stress only affects the elderly, but this study proves that young, healthy adults experience significant inflammatory changes and biological aging,” said Yu, who led the study. 

At 40, Vilas Kamble is far from elderly, yet the farmer from Maharashtra’s Kerle village in India began experiencing recurring chest pain several years after being diagnosed with hypertension, or high blood pressure. Doctors found blockages in his arteries, and he underwent coronary artery bypass surgery last year. Now he avoids working during the hottest parts of the day whenever possible.

Kamble cultivates rice, sugarcane, corn, and other crops, often spending more than seven hours outdoors doing physically demanding work. 

“Earlier, it never used to get so hot in our region,” he said.

Reducing risk in a warming world

Riggs said access to cooling, hydration, shade, and limiting heavy exertion during hot periods can help reduce heat strain. However, individual actions alone are not enough. 

“More broadly, stronger heat warning systems, occupational protections, and cooler built environments are all important,” he said. 

He noted that older adults, outdoor workers, people with cardiovascular, kidney, or metabolic disease, and those taking medications that affect hydration or thermoregulation are more vulnerable.

Yadav must farm to earn a living, so avoiding heat is easier said than done. Since his heart attack, he now tries to finish farmwork before noon and avoids working during the hottest part of the day. He has also stopped taking on additional work in construction. His wife, Lalita Yadav, 42, says she takes more frequent breaks and tries to limit prolonged heat exposure. 

“Now you can’t pass summers peacefully,” Yadav said. “Earlier, summers were meant to rest. But now, they have become extremely dangerous.”

MORE LIKE THIS

Summer heat could be hurting your heart without you knowing it

Workers are more likely to be injured on very hot days

People are getting financial incentives to use less electricity during heat waves
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: The hidden ocean world that helps regulate our climate
item.description:
Transcript:

Hundreds of feet deep in the ocean, light fades to black and a vibrant ecosystem comes to life.

This is the ocean’s twilight zone, a cold, dark place that’s home to strange jellyfish and glowing squids as well as plankton and countless fish.

Dorsett: “It’s really an amazing assemblage of animals that have adapted to these low light and cold conditions.”

Chris Dorsett of the nonprofit Ocean Conservancy says these animals help regulate the Earth’s climate by transporting planet-warming carbon deep underwater.

The ocean absorbs a lot of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Then tiny phytoplankton near the ocean’s surface take up carbon as they photosynthesize and grow.

Every night, creatures from the twilight zone swim to the surface to eat these phytoplankton and other creatures. Then they take that carbon back down to the deep, where much of it will remain.

But Dorsett says the twilight zone is in trouble as companies grow more interested in deep-sea fishing and mining.

So he says it’s vital that countries enact policies to protect it.

Dorsett: “This area very much needs to be protected before activities begin that could really impact it, and impact those values for society.”

Reporting credit: Ethan Freedman / ChavoBart Digital Media
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Tropical Storm Bertha is born, still crawling in the Gulf, with strong wind shear impacting it
item.description:
Update (Monday, July 20, 8:30 p.m. EDT):

The National Hurricane Center has officially upgraded Tropical Depression Two to Tropical Storm Bertha in the 8:00 PM EDT advisory. NOAA Hurricane Hunters flew into the system throughout the afternoon and found that the depression had strengthened into a tropical storm. It currently has maximum sustained winds of 40 mph as it continues to crawl west-northwest at around 5 mph (6 km/h). The minimum central pressure has also dropped to 1004 millibars (29.65 inches of mercury). This is slightly lower than Bertha’s central pressure during Monday afternoon.

[Haz clic aquí para leer en español]

Bertha arrived just three days after the average date of the Atlantic’s second named storm of the season (July 17), based on the period 1991-2020. In contrast, the Eastern Pacific produced its sixth named storm of 2026, Fausto, on Sunday, July 19, more than two weeks earlier than average.

Tropical-storm-force winds could begin impacting the western portion of the Florida Panhandle coast Monday evening into Tuesday morning. Expect tropical-storm-force winds to mainly impact the immediate coastline from Panama City Beach to Pensacola.

Remember that the system will be moving very slowly. Therefore, tropical-storm-force winds will gradually increase throughout the day Tuesday along the Mississippi and Alabama coasts. Then, by Tuesday night, wind gusts will increase as the system moves closer to the southeastern portion of Louisiana, specifically Plaquemines Parish.

There are no changes in the Tropical Storm Watches currently in effect as of the early evening advisory. However, additional watches and warnings may be required later tonight or on Tuesday.

During this afternoon, Hurricane Hunters noticed that the center had been moving erratically for most of the day due to its position between the mid-level ridges. Hurricane Hunters are scheduled to fly the system again overnight, with three additional reconnaissance missions scheduled between Tuesday and Wednesday. Keep in mind that because the center is moving erratically, there could be slight shifts in the forecast track. Also, very strong wind shear is impacting and will continue to impact Bertha, limiting further intensification quickly, and keeping the most intense rainfall to the south of the storm for now. We will continue to monitor the system closely.

Original article, posted on Monday, July 20:

After what seemed like rapid development from Tropical Depression Two, the system has slowed in organization over the last 24 hours. Much of its lack of organization is due to dry air intruding into the system from the northeast, which prevented it from becoming better organized overnight Sunday. Wind shear also appears to be increasing and is expected to remain strong, around 25 knots, through Tuesday, which will keep this system from intensifying quickly while it crawls over the northeastern Gulf.

Weather pattern keeping TD 2 crawling

The high-pressure system centered over the central United States will continue to steer the storm, which will get the name Bertha if it intensifies to tropical storm status, primarily westward this week. Another area of high pressure over the western Atlantic is preventing the tropical system from being pulled northward by the trough currently digging across the eastern United States. As these two high-pressure systems sandwich the tropical depression, the storm has very little steering flow. Over the weekend, TD 2 became nearly stationary, and as of Monday morning, it was moving northwest at only about 3 mph (6 km/h). This slow movement is expected to continue through at least late Tuesday afternoon. Once TD 2 begins to accelerate, we expect it to make a more pronounced westward turn.

The National Hurricane Center‘s cone of uncertainty remains quite broad, with the forecast center ranging from a landfall near the southeastern Louisiana coast on Wednesday afternoon to a track that keeps it about 100 miles south of the coastline. If the center takes the southern track and remains over water, it could mean a bit more strengthening as waters are plenty warm between 85 and 87 degrees F (29 to 30 degrees C).

This will be an all-week system

The latest official forecast from the National Hurricane Center brings future Tropical Storm Bertha ashore over the marshes of eastern Plaquemines Parish before it slowly tracks across coastal Louisiana on Wednesday and Thursday. The system could then re-emerge over the far northwestern Gulf of Mexico, where it may make a second landfall as a tropical depression somewhere along the southeast Texas coast by late this week or early next weekend.

Tropical Storm Watches remain in effect from the Florida Panhandle westward to Plaquemines Parish in Louisiana. These will likely be upgraded to Tropical Storm Warnings later today or on Tuesday. There is also a Storm Surge Watch in effect from the Alabama/Florida Border to the Mouth of the Mississippi River, Louisiana. Winds are expected to begin increasing Monday night and continue through Tuesday across the watch area. Keep in mind that TD 2 will likely expand its wind field as it moves slowly. Storms that struggle with dry-air intrusion often compensate by becoming larger. Also remember that although moving slowly, the system will continue to gain latitude over the next day or two. As a result, persistent onshore winds will continue to produce hazardous surf and rough marine conditions along Florida’s west coast through the middle of the week.

The system’s west-northwest motion will also keep onshore winds blowing from the Florida Panhandle through the central Gulf Coast for much of the week. As a result, storm surge combined with several cycles of high tide will likely produce coastal flooding as water is pushed inland. Areas from the Florida Panhandle through the central Gulf Coast, including Mobile Bay, could experience up to 3 feet of storm surge.

The storm’s slow movement will also lead to prolonged periods of heavy rainfall across the Gulf Coast states. Rainfall totals could easily exceed 6 inches in isolated locations throughout the week.

TD 2 will bring drought-easing rains to Louisiana and the Florida Panhandle

The National Hurricane Center is forecasting the second tropical system of the season to bring rains of 6 inches for portions of southern Louisiana and the Florida Panhandle. While rains of this magnitude could well cause damaging flooding, they will also be beneficial: the Gulf Coast has been suffering from long-term drought, and a significant deluge is required to break it. In southern Louisiana, NOAA’s latest Drought Termination and Amelioration map (Fig. 3) shows that southern Louisiana needs 7-11 inches of rain in just one month to bring it out of long-term drought; the Florida panhandle needs about 14 inches. The place that REALLY needs the rain is the Florida peninsula, where 22-25 inches of rain is needed in one month to break its long-term drought. Unfortunately, rains are predicted to miss the Florida peninsula; less than an inch of rain is currently forecast for it over the next seven days.

Florida has received rain from the tropical system’s rainbands, but it is not enough to dent the deficit. Across west-central Florida, as highlighted by Bob Henson during his weekend coverage of Tropical Depression Two, rainfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches in Sarasota and Venice, while three-day totals just west of St. Petersburg reached between 6 and 7 inches.

As future Bertha slowly moves away, rain bands will gradually diminish across the Florida Peninsula. However, heavier clusters of rain are expected to spread into the Florida Panhandle, along the Alabama and Mississippi coasts, and into southern Louisiana.

Is it unusual for a storm to move westward across the Gulf?

As long as the atmospheric pattern supports it, tropical cyclones can move in directions that may seem unusual. For a westward motion like this to occur (as opposed to the more common northwestward paths), there is typically a strong subtropical ridge positioned north of the storm. This ridge blocks the cyclone from making its natural turn northward while steering currents remain weak, forcing the system farther west.

Once it gets named, Tropical Storm Bertha will join a relatively short list of storms that have formed over the eastern Gulf of Mexico and moved westward. Tropical Storm Fernand in 2019 was one of the most recent examples. Although it did not actually form in the eastern Gulf, it followed a similar westward track before making landfall in northeastern Mexico on September 4, 2019.

An even more comparable track was Hurricane Erika in 2003, which crossed much of the Gulf from east to west. Erika formed on August 14 from the remnants of a frontal boundary, strengthened into a hurricane on August 15, and made landfall near Tamaulipas, Mexico, on August 16 as a Category 1 hurricane with maximum sustained winds of 75 mph (120 km/h).

Jeff Masters and Bob Henson contributed to this post.

MORE LIKE THIS

Tropical Storm Bertha makes its first landfall in southeastern Louisiana

Tropical Storm Bertha is born, still crawling in the Gulf, with strong wind shear impacting it

You can save lives this hurricane season
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: You can save lives this hurricane season
item.description:
Dear readers,

As we head into peak Atlantic hurricane season, I’m proud to be part of a team that doesn’t shy away from straightforwardly explaining how climate change is making these storms more dangerous. At a time when many news outlets are laying off their climate reporters, we’re more determined than ever to keep telling the truth. That’s why we’re asking readers like you to help us keep our extreme weather coverage going and growing.

I’m thrilled to announce that a generous Eye on the Storm reader has agreed to match your contribution dollar-for-dollar, up to $10,000. That means that if you donate by August 1, every dollar you contribute will become $2.

Donate

Anything you can spare is appreciated, but if you become a monthly donor at $15 a month or make a one-time donation of $50, that would make a huge difference in helping more people get lifesaving information about dangerous hurricanes, wildfires, heat waves, floods — and the role that climate change is playing in all of them.

“I trust this site to be upfront and honest with scientific data on what is happening to our planet,” one Eye on the Storm reader wrote. “It is alarming. I dread hurricane season, but I always look to this site.”

If you become a monthly donor between now and Saturday, August 1, you’ll be invited to join an exclusive, monthly-donors-only conversation with Jeff Masters, Irene Sans, and me over Zoom later this season. To sign up, make sure you’ve selected the “recurring” option on the donation form. (Note: If you set your donation to be anonymous, we won’t have your information to invite you to the conversation.)

We receive no direct funding from Yale University, so your support makes all the difference.

If you’d rather donate by check, please make it out to “Yale University” and write “For Yale Climate Connections” on the check. Mail it to: Yale Climate Connections, Yale University, Attn: Development Office, 205 Prospect Street, New Haven, CT 06511

Our federal tax ID is 06-0646973.

Donate

Thanks for your support,

Sara Peach

Editor-in-Chief

Yale Climate Connections
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Kids plant trees, change neighborhoods
item.description:
Transcript:

Children in the Pittsburgh area are helping improve their neighborhoods by planting trees.

The One Tree Per Child Program, run by the nonprofit Tree Pittsburgh, helps children plant trees around schools, parks, streets, playgrounds, and other spots.

Fantazier: “We really love when we’re able to plant trees in a space that those students are going to experience again and again. … Typically, it’s pretty magical. Young people often want to name the tree that they plant.”

Jonathan Fantazier, who runs the program, says the One Tree Per Child Initiative was started in Australia by singer Olivia Newton-John and her friend Jon Dee.

It was brought to Pittsburgh about 10 years ago, and participants have since planted hundreds of trees.

Fantazier’s team also hosts field trips at their tree nursery and helps students learn to care for trees.

Trees filter stormwater and provide shade, so the program will help the students’ neighborhoods adapt as climate change brings more extreme heat and heavy rain.

Fantazier: “The best part for me of working with young people who are aware of and concerned about climate change is that we can demonstrably show them something practical that they can do about it.”

Reporting credit: Ethan Freedman / ChavoBart Digital Media
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado
